title: Self-Employed Mortgages in Canada
metaTitle: Self-Employed Mortgage Canada — BFS Programs in BC
metaDescription: Self-employed and BFS (business-for-self) mortgage programs across Canada. Stated-income, gross-up, alt-A and standard programs explained for incorporated and sole-prop owners.
intro: Self-employed Canadians are a quarter of the workforce but only a fraction of mortgage marketing. The right program — chosen against your specific structure — can mean the difference between a 5.99% rate and a 4.59% rate.

body.heading: Three ways lenders look at self-employed income
paragraphs: Standard income recognition uses your line 150 income from your last two T1 General returns (or T2 corporate returns if incorporated). Most accountants legally optimize this number low; lenders see the optimized number and qualify you accordingly. Result: you under-qualify.

Gross-up programs add 15% back to your declared income to reflect the tax-optimization that legitimately understates your real cash flow. This is the most common BFS pathway at A-lenders. Stated-income programs (mostly at B-lenders and credit unions) let you state a reasonable income for your industry, supported by 6–12 months of bank statements showing the cash flow.
bullets: Standard: line 150 from last 2 years of returns, no add-back
BFS gross-up: 15% added to declared income at most A-lenders
Stated-income: 6–12 months of bank statements, B-lender or credit union
Asset-based: significant non-RRSP investments can support qualification

body.heading: Which program suits you?
paragraphs: Sole proprietor with 2+ years of stable T1 income: standard or BFS gross-up at an A-lender. Incorporated for 2+ years with active T2 retained earnings: BFS gross-up plus possible add-back of retained earnings — depending on the lender's appetite. Newly self-employed (under 2 years): typically B-lender or credit union with stated-income program. Heavy retained-earnings holder: equity-based program where the corporate balance sheet supports the file.

whoItsFor: Are a sole proprietor with consistent self-employed income for 2+ years
Have a contract or freelance income with verifiable bank deposits
Have meaningful retained earnings or business assets
requirements: Last 2 years of T1 General returns (and T2 if incorporated)
Last 2 years of Notices of Assessment (NOAs)
Articles of incorporation and proof of active business (HST registration, business licence)
Last 6 months of business and personal bank statements

faqs.question: How long do I need to be self-employed?
faqs.answer: Most A-lender programs want a 2-year history. Some B-lenders and credit unions accept 1 year with strong bank statements. Brand-new businesses usually need to wait until they have a tax return.

faqs.question: I expense everything to lower my taxes — is this hurting my mortgage qualification?
faqs.answer: Yes, somewhat. The fix isn't to stop expensing legitimate costs — it's to use a lender program (BFS gross-up, stated income) that understands the tax-optimization gap. Talk to me before changing how you report income to your accountant.

faqs.question: Can I use my corporation's retained earnings as down payment?
faqs.answer: Yes — though the lender will want to see 90 days of seasoning in your personal account before closing, or an alternative source of qualified-funds verification. Plan ahead by 90+ days when pulling money from a corp.
